project_scope.method_summary: Use an LLM for interpretable high-level policy code and a derivative-free optimizer for low-level numeric control.
project_scope.primary_claim_to_test: Readable code policies can be generated and tuned to reach competitive Atari performance across multiple object-centric environments.

active_thesis_direction.primary_goal: Build a reproducible method, not a collection of hand-tuned one-off prompts.
active_thesis_direction.interpretability_target: The final agent for each game should be inspectable Python/JAX policy code with a small parameter dictionary.
active_thesis_direction.performance_target: Push each policy as close as possible to neural baselines while preserving interpretability and reproducibility.
active_thesis_direction.scientific_target: Track when LeGPS succeeds, when it fails, whether failures come from the LLM, metrics, objectives, wrappers, or optimizer budget.

milestone_log.rule: When the thesis state changes meaningfully, add a new dated JSON milestone instead of overwriting previous snapshots.

new_game_onboarding: Add structured game context: objective, action mapping, object-centric observation layout, reward semantics, and known mechanics.
Run a single-game outer loop to find whether the unified method can produce a viable policy.
If performance is poor, diagnose the failure category before changing prompts or code.
Only fix infrastructure, context, metrics, or objective bugs when they are supported by evidence from code, metrics, or videos.
Promote the best policy to the canonical best-policy folder only after standardized reevaluation.
unified_regression_suite: After meaningful prompt or pipeline changes, rerun all covered games with the unified runner.
Compare the revised unified prompt against existing canonical best policies.
Treat regressions as method evidence, not as hidden failures.
Keep canonical artifacts separate from exploratory single-game runs.
reproducibility_requirements: Save policy code, parameters, seed, model name, provider, evaluation horizon, number of episodes, and runtime metadata.
Save exact LLM system prompts, user prompts, raw responses, and extracted outputs for future outer-loop runs.
Redact API keys in all saved artifacts.
Keep a single command path for rerunning canonical policy evaluation.
Use multiple seeded reruns before making strong claims about robustness.

research_questions.question: Can LeGPS produce successful RL agents?
research_questions.purpose: Establish that the method can produce working interpretable agents on original training tasks.
evidence_needed: Strong policies on the base games.
Coverage across multiple environments, not only one or two.
Comparison against verified random, human, and neural baselines when available.
current_evidence: Canonical 10000-step best policies exist for Pong, Freeway, Asterix, Breakout, and Skiing.
The documented canonical evaluation command reproduced all five stored scores exactly on a local macOS setup after API-free reproducibility-path fixes.
A first five-game seeded RQ1 rerun completed: Pong matched canonical performance, Breakout exceeded canonical, and Freeway, Asterix, and Skiing were below canonical.
Kangaroo is wired for exploratory LeGPS onboarding runs, but it does not yet have a canonical 10000-step policy.
The first Kangaroo LLM smoke run completed with valid artifacts and saved LLM input/output, but scored 0.0 in both the 1000-step smoke evaluation and a 10000-step API-free follow-up check.
The first Kangaroo failure was diagnosed as an UP-only controller with no horizontal movement, no reward events, and one life lost; Kangaroo logger metrics and a rollout renderer now exist.

The second Kangaroo smoke run completed with two LLM iterations and saved LLM input/output. It avoided pure UP-only behavior but policy_v2 overcorrected into horizontal-only dithering, still scoring 0.0.
After the second smoke run, Kangaroo route context was refined so prompt text, feedback, and demo code identify reachable ladders using player-bottom and ladder-bottom alignment.

The third Kangaroo smoke run produced the first nonzero Kangaroo score: policy_v2 averaged 100.0 in the short 1000-step run, but a 16-episode 10000-step API-free follow-up averaged only 12.5 with one scoring episode.

A resumed Kangaroo run produced policy_v3, which scored 200.0 in all eight 4000-step run-evaluation episodes and averaged 162.5 over a 16-episode 10000-step API-free follow-up. It is stronger than smoke3 but still plateaus at one 200-point reward event.

A second Kangaroo continuation produced policy_v6, which scored exactly 200.0 in all 16 episodes of a 10000-step follow-up. A rollout trace shows the 200-point event is a RIGHTFIRE reward at x=132,y=148, followed later by a life loss, so this is stable plateau evidence rather than a solved route.
A detailed post-200 trace for policy_v6 shows the life loss occurs when a falling coconut reaches x≈133,y≈129 while the player remains near x≈132,y≈132 after the 200-point event.
A targeted trace-guided Kangaroo continuation generated policy_v7, but it regressed to 0.0; the retained best LLM-run policy before manual patching remained policy_v6 at the stable 200-point plateau.
A high-budget policy_v6 evaluation over 128 episodes with a 50000-step cap stayed exactly at 200.0, and a smaller parameter-only CMA-ES retune also stayed at 200.0.
A debug overlay trace confirmed that policy_v6's on-ladder predicate is too permissive: 193 of 212 UP actions were triggered with only one pixel of player-ladder overlap.

A trace-guided API-free patch separated strict ladder-climb perception from route-column exclusion. The base patch-2 candidate averaged 2634.38 over 128 episodes with a 50000-step cap, and the local CMA-ES retuned patch-2 candidate averaged 2656.25 under the same high-budget protocol.

Exact replay of one 3600-point retuned patch-2 episode and one 200-point retuned patch-2 episode shows both traces contain zero UP actions. The high score comes from repeated lower-platform RIGHTFIRE monkey rewards, so the retuned patch-2 candidate is not yet a canonical covered-game policy.

Kangaroo prompt context and logger feedback now explicitly flag score-without-climbing and punch-farming behavior, so future outer-loop feedback should not treat high lower-platform punch reward as task success.

A follow-up anti-punch-farming Kangaroo outer loop completed, but all five generated rewrites scored 0.0 and the retained best remained policy_v6 at 200.0. The failure was diagnosed as overcorrecting the punch-farming warning by suppressing the first useful RIGHTFIRE reward branch.
Kangaroo logger and prompt feedback now distinguish punch farming from first-reward suppression using first_reward_suppressed_pattern and no_fire_scoreless_pattern.

A fresh anti-punch-overcorrection Kangaroo rerun completed with the same retained best of 200.0. One generated policy reached avg_return=75.0, but four of five new candidates were flagged as first-reward suppression, so Kangaroo remains exploratory.

Kangaroo continuation prompts now include a hard preservation contract for the stable 200-point baseline: preserve the lower-platform route, monkey-threat detection, danger/punch parameter semantics, and first RIGHTFIRE branch while modifying only post-200/top-of-ladder logic.

A fresh-from-scratch Kangaroo run using the revised architecture recovered a 200.0 policy without resuming from the previous best, but its 32-episode 10000-step follow-up averaged only 93.75, with 17 zero-score episodes and 15 episodes at 200.

Kangaroo now has an optional shaped route-progress search objective behind --kangaroo-shaped-objective. This is not canonical performance evidence yet; it is an explicitly labeled scaffold to test whether CMA-ES can escape the first-reward plateau while real reward remains the final reporting metric.

The first shaped-objective Kangaroo fresh run stabilized the first reward: policy_v2 averaged 193.75 over 32 episodes with a 10000-step cap using saved CMA-ES best_params, with 31 episodes at 200 and one at 0. It still had zero episodes above 200.

The shaped policy_v2 debug trace exactly explains all actions and shows post-200 failure comes from repeated monkey_in_punch_priority RIGHTFIRE overriding route/dismount behavior near the top ladder state.

Two local post-200 guard patches preserved the stable 200-point behavior but did not improve beyond it. Action probes show the policy reaches a y=132 ladder-top trap where no atomic action escapes before life loss.

A bounded top-ladder transition sequence probe tested 216 short forced-action candidates from the y=140 post-reward state. No candidate exceeded 200; the best short prefix, DOWNRIGHTx24 + DOWNLEFTx16, delayed failure but a longer replay still died at score 200 near x=126,y=132.
Current results include multiple human-normalized scores above or near human level.
Breakout remains meaningfully below DQN and is an important improvement target.

research_questions.question: Do LeGPS policies solve the intended task, or do they rely on brittle shortcuts?
research_questions.purpose: Check whether interpretable policies are genuinely aligned with the game objective instead of exploiting accidental simplifications or wrapper artifacts.
evidence_needed: Video and metric audits showing whether behavior matches the intended task.
Shortcut probes such as lazy opponents, altered obstacle layouts, or removed distractors.
Explicit failure classification when metrics and visual behavior disagree.
current_evidence: Pong and Freeway have prior video/mod inspection.
Asterix and Skiing exposed why metric/objective validation is necessary.

Kangaroo retuned patch-2 is a concrete example of misleading score improvement: one replay reaches 3600 points with 18 punch rewards and zero UP actions, meaning it does not demonstrate robust ladder navigation.
Kangaroo logger metrics now include score_without_climb_pattern and punch_farming_pattern to identify this shortcut automatically in future runs.

The later anti-punch-farming run is a complementary failure case: removing or broadly gating FIRE avoids punch farming but also destroys the known first reward, so RQ2 evaluation must distinguish shortcut prevention from task progress.
A second anti-punch rerun confirmed that preserving task-relevant sub-behaviors matters: preventing one shortcut is not enough if the generated policy destroys the first legitimate reward route.

The fresh Kangaroo run's best policy is not flagged as punch farming or first-reward suppression, but it still only reaches the shallow first-event route and never exceeds 200 in the 32-episode follow-up.

The shaped Kangaroo objective explicitly penalizes lower-platform punch farming without upward progress, but any resulting policy still needs video/debug inspection before it can count as intended-task behavior.

A trace and GIF of the shaped run's best policy show one RIGHTFIRE reward at x=132,y=148 followed by life loss at x=133,y=132, so it remains a shallow first-reward policy rather than a robust route solution.

The stack-style debug renderer now shows the exact logical stack and final reason per frame for shaped policy_v2; this supports diagnosing whether score comes from intended route logic or a priority shortcut.
The post-200 guard probes show the current failure is not only a punch-priority shortcut; it is also a top-of-ladder transition/mechanics failure that needs a targeted action-sequence probe.
The bounded top-ladder transition probe rules out a simple one- or two-block forced-action prefix as enough to solve the post-200 failure; the remaining problem is a staged route/mechanics problem.
A formal RQ2 test suite is not implemented yet.

research_questions.question: Can LeGPS policies be adapted efficiently to task variations?
research_questions.purpose: Measure whether existing symbolic policies can be modified for new goals or environment variants with less effort than starting from scratch.
evidence_needed: Task variants that change structure, goals, or constraints.
Adaptation runs initialized from current policy context.
Comparison of adaptation speed and final score against clean-from-scratch runs.
current_evidence: The mod infrastructure exists for several games.
The adaptation protocol is not implemented yet.

research_questions.question: How much performance comes from CMA-ES numeric optimization?
research_questions.purpose: Ablate the inner-loop optimizer to quantify whether LeGPS needs search or whether the LLM can set useful constants alone.
evidence_needed: Run the same outer loop with optimizer=none.
Prompt the LLM to choose final constants itself.
Compare LLM-only policies against full LeGPS with CMA-ES under the same evaluation budget.
current_evidence: An optimizer=none path is available for LLM-only ablation runs.
A first optimizer=none suite has been run on Pong, Freeway, Asterix, Breakout, and Skiing with 5 outer-loop iterations and 10000-step evaluations.
The first RQ4 result shows CMA-ES is especially important for Asterix and Breakout, moderately useful for Pong and Freeway, and nearly neutral on the current Skiing policy.
For Kangaroo only, an optional shaped search objective has been added. Any run using it must be interpreted separately from pure reward-only CMA-ES evidence.

deferred_or_uncertain: breakout is already covered despite being temporarily deprioritized earlier
seaquest may require richer context due oxygen, divers, enemies, and projectiles

known_methodological_risks.risk: Game-specific prompt engineering can weaken the unified-method claim.
known_methodological_risks.mitigation: Frame the method as one shared prompt scaffold plus structured game context; log all context changes.

known_methodological_risks.risk: Metrics or reward semantics can be wrong or misleading.
known_methodological_risks.mitigation: Audit each game objective, logger metrics, and video behavior before interpreting scores.
Run seeded reruns for the canonical games before final claims.

known_methodological_risks.risk: Manual debugging can turn the method into hidden hand engineering.
known_methodological_risks.mitigation: Separate infrastructure/context fixes from final policy search, and document why each fix was necessary.

known_methodological_risks.risk: Repeated targeted rewrites on one game can overfit effort to a single environment while reducing game coverage.
known_methodological_risks.mitigation: Document regressions, freeze partial progress when returns diminish, and move to the next game when broader coverage is the current thesis priority.

known_methodological_risks.risk: A shaped objective can improve the optimizer signal while weakening the purity of reward-only evaluation.
known_methodological_risks.mitigation: Use shaping only as an explicitly labeled Kangaroo scaffold, keep real reward as the final reported metric, and separate shaped runs from canonical evidence.

next_milestones.description: Re-evaluate all saved best policies from the canonical folder and compare against stored scores.
Run optimizer=none for all current games and compare against full LeGPS.
Run multiple seeds per current game to estimate variance in policy generation and optimization.
Define task simplifications and shortcut tests for the current games.

Completed first pass, stack inspection, local guard patches, and a bounded top-ladder sequence probe. The current blocker remains a top-of-ladder transition trap; no short tested forced-action prefix exceeded 200.
Treat Kangaroo as partial exploratory progress for now and onboard Seaquest through the single-game protocol.

working_reminder.instruction: Prioritize thesis-valid evidence over isolated score chasing. Every new result should map to RQ1, RQ2, RQ3, or RQ4.
